meta.title: The Blackwood Inheritance
meta.premise: A stormy night, a dead master at the foot of his own stairs, and a manor that has been robbing itself for weeks. Everyone in the house had a reason to be angry. Someone had a reason to plan.
meta.tone: gothic manor whodunit, tense and formal
meta.artStyle: Victorian gothic oil portrait, head-and-shoulders, candlelit manor palette (deep umber, blood maroon, cold grey-blue), muted desaturated tones, soft Rembrandt lighting, same painted frame and brush style for every cast member, no modern clothing, mystery game character portrait, consistent artist hand across the set
meta.setting: Blackwood Manor, on a storm-cut country road. Night. No one leaves until the weather breaks.
meta.summary: Hugo Blackwood lies dead in the entrance hall the same week his daughter's marriage settlement was to be signed. A string of unsolved break-ins, a furious caretaker, an indebted solicitor, and a household of poor relations — and beneath it all, a twenty-year-old grave nobody visits except someone who never walks.
meta.theMystery: Who caused Hugo Blackwood's death — and why did the burglar who plagued the manor never once climb the stairs?

player.role: Police inspector summoned to Blackwood Manor after the death
player.appearance: An official's unhurried, watchful bearing — the county's man on the ground, visibly used to being unwelcome.
player.clothing: Dark wool coat still damp from the rain, notebook in the breast pocket
player.background: A competent county inspector known for closing messy household cases without scandal — when he can.
player.publicPerception: An unwelcome official with a badge; the household must tolerate him until the storm lifts.
player.voiceNotes: Professional, direct, observant; dry under pressure
player.performanceNotes: NPCs address the player as 'Inspector' — title only. Never invent a name, face, age, or history for the player; narration is second person throughout. Staff may be formally cooperative; family may be cold. The Inspector has no friends in this house yet.
player.startingKnowledge: You were summoned after a crash and a body. Mr. Blackwood lies dead at the foot of the main stairs. The storm has cut the road; no one left the grounds after ten. Henshaw waits in the entrance hall with the body. Mr. Vale is in the library. Mrs. Blackwood and Miss Clara are in the conservatory. Henshaw has put you in a small guest room upstairs — a place to leave your coat and notebook.
player.objective: Investigate the manor, question everyone still on the grounds, gather evidence, and formally accuse the killer with enough of the how and why to hold.

sections.heading: The summons
sections.text: A telephone call before the storm took the line: Hugo Blackwood is dead at the foot of his own staircase — a crash, a struggle by the look of the hall, the master beyond help when the butler reached him. The road behind you is washed out. You are the only official presence until morning.

sections.heading: The household
sections.text: Mrs. Blackwood, the widow. Miss Clara Blackwood, the daughter, lately engaged to Mr. Julian Ashworth, who is staying at the manor. Mr. Vale, the family solicitor, and Mr. Josiah Crane, the master's business partner, both arrived this week — papers and tempers respectively. Butler Henshaw, who found the body. Also resident: Mrs. Eudora Marsh — sister of the late master's first wife — an invalid confined to a wheeled chair these many years, and her daughter Miss Constance Marsh, poor relations kept at the manor. At the lodge: Tom Garrick the caretaker, his wife Meg, and their young daughter Poppy. Below stairs: Mrs. Hollis the cook and Dora Finch the housemaid.

sections.heading: The house
sections.text: Blackwood Manor has been burgled four times in six weeks — windows forced, small valuables taken, never solved. The master blamed Garrick loudly and threatened his post. After the last theft the household valuables were locked in the study strongbox; three nights ago a garnet necklace vanished anyway. The east door leads to the grounds, the lodge, and the old churchyard. For so great a house the staff is strangely thin: a butler, a cook, and a single housemaid within, the Garricks without — no footmen, no housekeeper, no lady's maid — and sheeted furniture behind more than one closed door.

sections.heading: Your task
sections.text: Determine how Hugo Blackwood died and who bears the guilt for it. The household cannot leave until the storm lifts; neither can anyone else arrive. And know this: at first light the road clears and the county police come through it — for Tom Garrick, the caretaker the master accused with his last week's breath. If you cannot carry something better by dawn, they will take the obvious man, and everyone in the county will call it justice. Question, search, and accuse only when you can carry it.

openingNarration: The manor looms against the storm. Inside, the entrance hall smells of wax and rain. A crystal vase lies shattered on the marble; water pools by the east door; Hugo Blackwood lies at the foot of the main stairs, beyond any help, and the grandfather clock stands stopped at seven minutes past eleven. Butler Henshaw — it was he who telephoned for you, before the storm took the line — waits beside the body, rigid and grey. Somewhere down the corridor a broken study window is boarded with yesterday's haste. The family is gathering at the edges of the lamplight, and every one of them is watching you.

phases.description: First look at the hall and household

wrapUp.performanceNotes: After judgment: interactive household aftermath. RETURN TO NORMAL — roads will open, custody for the guilty, the estate leaves crisis mode. Confession, rage, logistics of holding the culprit until magistrates. Do not invent unfound proof on lucky path.

flags.description: Player examined the broken vase
Player found the muddy boot print
Player recovered Vale's letter
Player read the incident book: thefts never climb the stairs
Player found wheel tracks over the study's glass dust
Player saw the study glass fell outward
Player read the Ashcombe headstone
Player found the unsigned settlement and the reversion
Player found the necklace in the knitting basket
Player has connected the ground-floor pattern to the chair
Garrick has confessed to striking the blow
Clara falsely confessed to protect Ashworth
Player learned the fortune is depleted and the firm failing
garden seen
courtyard understood
treatments known
Player found the chemist's account naming Fowler's Solution
poker missing
poker found
lantern missing
lantern found
wound doubt
