Meg has unlocked Tom's soldier chest for the player
Player has learned Garrick's court-martial and discharge
Runtime: player formally accused Mrs. Marsh (engine-set)
Runtime: player formally accused Garrick (engine-set)

locations.name: Blackwood Manor — the entrance hall
locations.description: Marble floor, storm-lashed windows, the grandfather clock stopped at seven minutes past eleven, and a shattered crystal vase. The body has been covered. Half the sconces stand unlit and two of the great rooms off the gallery are sheeted and shut — a large house run by a very small staff. The east door leads to the grounds; the corridor runs to the library and study; stairs rise to the private rooms.

exits.label: the corridor to the library
the corridor to the study
the conservatory doors
the main stair up to the gallery
the east door to the grounds
the double doors to the dining room
the green baize door to the service passage
the parlor door to Mrs. Marsh's sickroom

onInspect.narrativeHints: Shards lie crushed and scattered — not swept from its stand but borne down under a falling weight. A dark wool thread is snagged on the jagged glass — fine cloth, a gentleman's coat.
A wet boot print points inward from the east door — a heavy working boot, size eleven, half washed away by the draft of rain.

Stopped dead at 11:07 — no chime hour, no accident of winding; the pendulum simply quit. The case is scarred low on one side where something heavy slammed against it in a struggle. Whatever happened in this hall happened at seven minutes past eleven, and the clock will swear to it.
Laid on the side table: a pocket watch, a signet, and a ring of keys. One small steel key is stamped for the study strongbox.
Henshaw's neat station: silver book, salver — and the household incident book, kept in his precise hand since the thefts began.

A single round, depressed wound at the right temple. The vase shards carry no blood. The stair edges are square, the newel post carries none. He fell backward; the wound faces sideways. State the geometry plainly and let it sit.

locations.name: Blackwood Manor — the library
locations.description: Cold and dark. A fire smolders. A ledger lies open on the desk to the week's accounts. Ash in the hearth glints with metal.

exits.label: back to the entrance hall

onInspect.narrativeHints: A brass key glints in the ash — still warm enough that it was dropped tonight.

The brass key turns. Inside: a letter signed by Mr. Vale, dated Tuesday: 'If you expose me I will have no choice. Give me until the quarter-day. — V.' Beneath it, the ledger notes a settlement drawn for the wedding.

The estate ledger, open to the quarter's accounts — and the figures do not balance. Sums drawn against 'the settlement fund' in the master's hand, again and again. Beneath the ledger, a bundle of letters from Blackwood & Crane.
The stand holds tongs, brush, and shovel. No poker. Henshaw, asked, goes still: the set was complete, he would swear it — until that night.

locations.name: Blackwood Manor — the study
locations.description: The burgled room. A boarded window, a fine powder of glass dust across the floor boards, a heavy strongbox in the corner, and the master's papers squared on the desk. Curiously little broken glass in here for so violent an entry.

exits.label: back to the entrance hall

onInspect.narrativeHints: Forced three nights ago, they say — yet only a fine dust of glass lies on this side; no shards, no panes. The weight of the glass went somewhere. Outside, the flowerbed might tell the story.
In the glass dust, two narrow parallel tracks run from the door to the window and back — thin wheels, pressed OVER the dust. A chair was in this room after the window broke.

An old strongbox — older than the second marriage; the Ashcombe crest is worn on the lid. The steel key fits. Inside: the Ashcombe settlement of 1888. Rosalind Ashcombe's fortune, held on her death by Hugo Blackwood as CUSTODIAN for the Ashcombe line — to pass to her sister Eudora upon remarriage, or to her niece Constance upon marriage, or to the surviving Ashcombe line upon the custodian's death. Beneath it, Vale's fresh draft: a marriage portion for Miss Clara — to be drawn from the settlement fund.

Household receipts, squared and clipped. Among them a chemist's standing account, renewed monthly for years: 'Fowler's Solution, to be made up at double strength, for Mrs. Marsh — per Mr. Blackwood's standing instruction.' No physician's name appears anywhere on it.

locations.name: Blackwood Manor — the conservatory
locations.description: Rain roars on the glass. Ferns, wicker, a day couch, and a neat corner arranged for an invalid's chair — a ramp of smooth board at the step, everything wanted within an arm's easy reach.

exits.label: back to the entrance hall
the ramped door out to the side courtyard

onInspect.narrativeHints: Mrs. Marsh's domain: rug, footstool, a small shelf of devotional books. The ramp boards are worn smooth with years of wheels. From here the whole ground floor is hers.
Beneath the wool, wrapped in a child's handkerchief: a garnet necklace. Old-fashioned, beautiful — and stolen from the study strongbox three nights ago.

locations.name: Blackwood Manor — your guest room
locations.description: A small neat room upstairs where Henshaw put your coat and bag. The window looks over the east grounds: the lodge's lit window, and beyond it the dark shoulder of the old churchyard.

exits.label: the passage back to the gallery landing

onInspect.narrativeHints: From here the geography is plain: east door below, the gravel path to the lodge, the churchyard wall. Anyone moving between them at night would pass under this window — and under the lodge's.

locations.name: the east grounds
locations.description: Wind and rain. The east terrace runs beneath the study windows; gravel paths lead to the caretaker's lodge and the old churchyard. The storm has half-drowned every track. Where the paths fork, a tool lean-to stands against the garden wall.

exits.label: in through the east door
the path to the caretaker's lodge
the path to the old churchyard
around the house to the side courtyard

inspectables.name: the flowerbed under the study window

onInspect.narrativeHints: Glass. Nearly all of it lies OUT here, glittering among the stems — thrown outward when the pane broke. Whoever broke that window stood inside the study when they did it.
The lock is sound; the door was opened, not forced. Deep heel prints in the mud face the house — a big man, moving fast, tonight.

inspectables.name: the tool lean-to by the churchyard path

onInspect.narrativeHints: A groundsman's lean-to, tools hung in rough order — rakes, shears, a scythe. The long spade hangs wrong-way-about, wiped in haste rather than cleaned: dark churchyard clay dried hard in the seam of the socket. It comes off its nail easily. Nobody is watching.

locations.name: the caretaker's lodge
locations.description: Small, warm, scrubbed. A gun rack by the door, a child's drawings pinned over the stove, and fear in the room like a smell. The Garricks stop talking when you enter. A brass-bound soldier's chest sits beneath the bed, padlocked.

exits.label: back along the path to the grounds

inspectables.name: the window by the child's bed

onInspect.narrativeHints: A clear line of sight to the manor's east face: the study windows, the terrace, the east door. A child lying awake would see any light that moved down there.
Working boots, size eleven, still wet through. Nobody has tried to hide them. Meg Garrick watches you look and her hands go still.

An empty iron hook by the door, rubbed bright by years of the same handle — and a cheap stable candle stub standing on the shelf where a good dark lantern should hang. A groundsman doing storm rounds by candle stub: everyone has noticed, nobody has wondered.

inspectables.name: the soldier's chest beneath the bed

onInspect.narrativeHints: The lock is open now. Inside, folded small: a court-martial record and discharge sheet — Sergeant Thomas Garrick, 66th Foot, reduced and drummed out for striking a lieutenant. Beneath it, a medal ribbon nobody could quite throw away and a browning regimental photograph. And balled into the corner, linen bandages balled small, the blood on them still fresh — a wound at shoulder height, bound in haste and in secret, tonight.

locations.name: the old churchyard
locations.description: A low-walled churchyard on the grounds, older than the manor's present name. Yews, leaning stones, and one corner kept bare and plain while the Blackwood monument gleams. Rain runs on the letters. By the boundary wall, a mound of fresh-turned earth stands dark in the rain — far too small for a grave, and somehow the worse for that.

exits.label: back along the path to the grounds

inspectables.name: the plain headstone in the corner

onInspect.narrativeHints: 'ROSALIND BLACKWOOD, née ASHCOMBE. 1858–1889. Fell asleep.' A first wife no one mentions — dead at thirty-one, the year before the master remarried. Fresh flowers lie against the stone, beaten by the rain but placed this week.
Marble, recent, self-satisfied. Space marked out for Hugo Blackwood and his second family. Nothing connects it to the plain stone in the corner — by design, one feels.

The rain has packed the soil hard as a floor; the spade goes through it in minutes. A foot down, wrapped in sacking: a dark lantern, iron and brass, its side deeply dented, scrubbed clean and buried wet. Fresh earth in a churchyard draws no eye — that was the whole idea.

locations.name: the side courtyard
locations.description: A small paved court on the sheltered side of the house, reached by a ramped door from the conservatory. An old balcony juts above, its window sheeted and shut for decades. In the coldest corner, someone keeps a little garden — tended with great care, and one bed of it strangely blighted.

exits.label: the ramped door into the conservatory
around the house to the east grounds

onInspect.narrativeHints: Low beds an arm's reach from a chair, tended daily by the look of them. One corner is dead — the soil sour, the plants burned brown in a neat splash pattern, as if something is poured out here, often, and has been for a long time.

Beneath two loose stones at chair height: a cache of small bottles — the tonic the master so attentively carried up each evening. A year of doses, perhaps more, every one full and unopened, the chemist's labels all reading FOWLER'S SOLUTION. And wrapped beside them, a poor woman's few jewels — the very pieces reported stolen in the attack on Mrs. Marsh.

The balcony belongs to the shut-up rooms — the first Mrs. Blackwood's, by the age of the sheeting glimpsed through the glass. The drop from the rail ends exactly where the little garden grows. The flagstones below carry a mason's old patch, decades weathered.

locations.name: Mrs. Marsh's room — the converted parlor
locations.description: A ground-floor parlor made over into a sickroom years ago: bed, chair-ramp, a shelf of devotional books, a full bottle of tonic standing untouched on the nightstand. One window is newly mended — the attack, eight days ago. It is austere as a servant's room, and it is forty feet from the entrance hall.

exits.label: the parlor door to the entrance hall

onInspect.narrativeHints: Reglazed after the attack. Ask the glazier's boy, or look at the flowerbed outside: the old glass fell OUTWARD here too — the same signature as the study. Broken from within.

A poor relation's inventory: darned shawls, her late husband's photograph, a jewel box standing open and empty since the attack. The tonic bottle on the nightstand is full, its seal unbroken, in plain sight of anyone who ever visited. No one visits.

Flat on the boards under an invalid's bed — the one place in this house no one would presume to search — lies the library fire poker. Dried blood and a shred of dark wool cling to its working end: not the dead man's blood — a temple wound bleeds down a face, not onto a poker — but the mark of a hard blow through heavy cloth, at the height of a big man's shoulder.

locations.name: The Gallery Landing
locations.description: The first-floor landing, running as a gallery above the entrance hall. Family bedroom doors stand closed along the passage — private rooms, and the household means them to stay so. Portraits line the rail. At the far end, the door to the shut-up wing: locked these seven years, sheeted furniture dim beyond its glass panes. From the rail you look straight down on the spot where the master lay.

exits.label: the main stair down to the hall
the passage to your guest room
the narrow back stair up to the attics

onInspect.narrativeHints: The master's own portrait is the newest and much the largest — commissioned, by the varnish, within the decade. The second family glows around it: his wife, Miss Clara on a pony. Of anything earlier there is no portrait at all. It is a wall with a careful absence in it.

locations.name: The Dining Room
locations.description: A long dark table cleared to its cloth, chairs squared, the master's place at the head. The sideboard carries the household plate behind glass. The room smells of beeswax and of dinners nobody has eaten since Thursday.

exits.label: the double doors to the entrance hall
the service door to the kitchen passage

inspectables.name: the sideboard and the household plate

onInspect.narrativeHints: The plate is complete — Henshaw could recite it: salvers, candlesticks, the fish service, every last spoon. Four burglaries in this house, and not a fork of it ever touched.

locations.name: The Kitchen
locations.description: Mrs. Hollis's kingdom: scoured deal table, copper pans in ranks, the range banked and ticking. Warm, floury, and the only room in the house that still sounds like ordinary life. In the corner, a stone stair drops to the cellars.

exits.label: the green baize door to the hall
the service door to the dining room
the stone stair down to the cellars

onInspect.narrativeHints: Everything in its place and everything spotless. The bread Mrs. Hollis was proving at eleven that night rose over its bowl and was baked anyway at dawn — because what else is there to do.

locations.name: The Cellars
locations.description: Whitewashed vaults under the kitchen: wine bins, the coal store, hams in muslin, a smell of damp stone and candle. Cold enough to see your breath.

exits.label: the stone stair up to the kitchen

inspectables.name: the bins and the coal store

onInspect.narrativeHints: Dust lies on the bin-tops in a smooth grey field, weeks deep and nowhere disturbed. Whatever has been happening in this house, none of it happened down here.

locations.name: The Attics
locations.description: Low rooms under the slates: the maid's cot and washstand, a gurgling cistern, moth-eaten curtains over lumber. Rain drums close overhead. Poppy's age would find it wonderful; everyone else finds it cold.

exits.label: the back stair down to the gallery

onInspect.narrativeHints: Outgrown clothes, cracked crockery, curtain rings, a dressmaker's dummy that startles you exactly once. Ordinary lumber of an ordinary household — nothing here has been touched since summer.

relationships.label: long service; protects the household's name
owes the estate money; quarrelled over exposure
relationships.notes: The engineered first suspicion — real, but not murder.

relationships.label: engaged; settlement to be signed this week
treats her cousin as a charity case
blamed for the thefts; post threatened
will lie for her husband without hesitation
twenty years of silent certainty about her sister's death
relationships.notes: The case's taproot. Revealed by the headstone and the trust.

relationships.label: everything, in the end, is for her daughter
thinks the master's blame was unjust
partner watching Blackwood sink the firm
together over the accounts at eleven

characters.name: Mr. Hugo Blackwood
characters.shortBio: Master of the manor — dead at the foot of his own stairs.

knowledge.public: Deceased. Found by Henshaw minutes after a crash was heard.

characters.name: Mrs. Blackwood
characters.shortBio: The widow. Composed until she is not. A brewery heiress who married Hugo five years ago and adored him without reservation: to her he was magnanimous, successful, open-handed — and to her he truly was. He raised her daughter as his own. No unkind fact about him ever crossed her threshold. Runs the house by lists and grieves in inventories, because collapse is not in her vocabulary.

knowledge.public: She was in her rooms when the crash came. She speaks of her husband in superlatives that are not performance: the most generous man she ever knew, who took her daughter as his own without being asked. She endured the burglaries with patience because Hugo asked it of her. Ask about her household and she will mention, with real reproach, that Mrs. Marsh refuses her tonic — after all the pains dear Hugo took over it, carrying it up himself every evening — and that her decline is largely her own doing.

private.content: The stolen necklace was Rosalind's garnet — the first wife's. Hugo gave it to her on their wedding as if it were new. She wore it seldom; it never felt hers. But the giving was so like him — nothing was ever too good for her.

'The fortune came with the first marriage — Hugo never hid that from me. But he could not speak of her. He avoided the churchyard corner; he could hardly bear poor Mrs. Marsh's face at his table. I thought it the grief of a man who feels more than he can say. He gave so freely — to Clara, to me, to the parish. A man who gives like that has nothing to hide, Inspector. Only things he cannot bear.'
'Hugo took to keeping the poker by him of an evening after poor Eudora was attacked. I thought it theatrics. Hugo was not a strong man, Inspector — whatever he meant to do with it, God knows.'

'Hugo never had a word against Garrick — six years, not one. These last weeks he talked of turning the family out entirely. I don't know what changed in him. The burglaries, I suppose. Something kept them always fresh in his mind.'

defenses: Grief in private only
Answers exactly what is asked
Meets any imputation against Hugo with genuine, wounded disbelief — grief, not calculation; she simply never saw the man the ledgers knew

characters.name: Miss Clara Blackwood
characters.shortBio: Daughter of the house, lately engaged to Mr. Ashworth. Nineteen, raised to be ornamental, and better with horses than with drawing rooms. Loves Ashworth chiefly because he talks to her as if she were sensible. On rainy afternoons she teaches the caretaker's little girl her letters — Papa teased her for it, 'my little schoolmistress,' and then paid for the primers. She was his jewel and knew it; no version of Papa but the generous one was ever shown to her.
characters.voice: Young, strained, sharper than she means

knowledge.public: Engaged three weeks; the settlement was to be signed Friday. She makes no secret of finding her cousin Constance a burden on the household. She worshipped her father and will hear nothing against him.

private.content: She overheard her father and Vale arguing Tuesday about money and 'exposure.'

'My cousin is a sponger — she and her mother have lived on Papa's charity for years. Papa never once complained of the expense; I doubt he even counted it. And the ingratitude — that woman will not so much as take the medicine he troubled over every single evening.'

defenses: Protects Ashworth fiercely
Will lie badly if he is threatened
Any slight on Papa is met with fury, then tears — both entirely real

characters.name: Mr. Julian Ashworth
characters.shortBio: Clara's fiancé, staying at the manor for the settlement week. A junior barrister of good family and modest means, earnest to the point of stiffness. He knows perfectly well the county assumes he is marrying the settlement rather than the girl, and the assumption wounds him more than he will admit — which makes him scrupulous about money to the point of comedy.
characters.voice: Well-bred, restless, a little too quick

knowledge.public: Arrived Monday for the signing of the marriage settlement. He and Blackwood argued about its terms on Tuesday, within earshot of half the house.

private.content: Blackwood demanded terms he found insulting — as though he were a fortune hunter to be fenced in. He said things he regrets. Half the house heard them.

The bitter joke: without the master's signature he gets nothing at all. The wedding portion dies with the man. If you want to know where the fortune goes instead, ask Vale — he went grey when I asked him that this morning.

defenses: Prickly about money
Honest when cornered

characters.name: Mrs. Eudora Marsh
characters.shortBio: The victim's aunt by his first marriage — Rosalind's elder sister, taken in as a poor relation and kept, with her daughter, on conspicuous charity. Dying, and knows it. Genuinely wheelchair-bound; genuinely gentle-voiced; genuinely fond of the caretaker's little girl. Answers only what is asked, thanks everyone for small kindnesses, and watches the household the way a clockmaker watches a mechanism she assembled. Nothing about her manner is false except everything it conceals.

knowledge.public: Widowed, wheelchair-bound these many years, resident at the manor with her daughter Constance; her room is a converted parlor on the ground floor, on account of the chair. Eight days ago a man came through her window in the night, took her few jewels, and had his hands at her throat before a crash of the storm startled him off — the household has been on a war footing since. She is also, the household will sigh, a difficult patient who will not take her medicine. She grieves correctly and completely.

private.content: Her account, given steadily and often: a big rough man, a working man's boots, the smell of wet outdoors on him; hands at her throat; saved when the storm burst a shutter and he fled. She is very exact about the boots. [SEALED: every word of this is her invention — the window broken from her own chair, the jewels wrapped beside her hoarded tonic. Perform it as unshakeable victim's testimony; the boots aim it at Garrick.]
'Rosalind was my sister. This was Ashcombe money and an Ashcombe house before it wore his name. You may read the stones in the churchyard if the family will not say it.'
'The doctors allow me until spring, perhaps less. At my age, Inspector, one arranges one's affairs and is not sentimental about it.'
'One sits in a chair in a corner and one is furniture, Inspector. Furniture hears everything. The quarrels, the letters, the sums. A house tells its bones to anyone who cannot leave it.'
'I keep the little garden in the side court. The cold corner of the house, yes. Someone should tend that spot, Inspector. Someone has to.'

secrets.content: Her composure opens one inch: 'My husband died of a convenient fever the year Constance was born, and the fund passed to Hugo. My sister came down from her own balcony four years later, and everything was his to hold. When I asked questions, I began to sicken — under his devoted care, his evening tonic — Fowler's Solution, for my 'nerves' — carried up by his own hand while the county called him a saint. I stopped drinking it eighteen months ago. My legs are gone and my heart is going, but my mind came back to me, Inspector, and I have kept that to myself. I could prove none of it. Not one death, not one dose. You cannot either.'

She looks at the necklace a long time, and then at the chair's wheels, and does not trouble to lie. 'The thefts were mine. My sister's keys still open her own house — the strongbox wore the Ashcombe crest before it ever held his papers, and its twin key has lived on my sister's ring these twenty years. I broke the study window from within and let the house believe in a burglar; I unlatched doors and let angry men be angry men in the dark. He put me in this chair, Inspector — it seemed just that the chair should answer him. His own tonic taught me patience. I never raised a hand against Hugo Blackwood; consider whether I could. The only blood on that poker is Tom Garrick's, and it was Hugo who put it there. I dealt the cards, Inspector — the night played them. I chose the soldier to be the one standing at the end, and if it had gone otherwise, there would have been another night. There was always going to be another night. And you will ask about the whispers. Little of what I told either man was even false — stolen things, an unsafe house, a displeased master. The burglar alone I invented, and him I described to each man in the shape of his own fear. Most of my instruments were true things, Inspector. Truth does murder quite willingly, if you deal it in the right order. I am dead by spring either way. The money goes home to Constance, where my sister meant it to be — if there is any of it left.'

defenses: Absolute composure
Gently returns every suspicion toward Mr. Vale's quarrel
Never volunteers; answers only what is asked
If Poppy's testimony is raised: 'Little Poppy dreams, Inspector. The whole house knows it. Ask her mother.'
If Constance's testimony is raised: sorrowful, unshaken — 'My daughter has listened for my chair all her life. She hears it in her sleep. You would hang a mother on a dream?'
If accused of turning Blackwood against Garrick: 'I told Hugo nothing but truths. Things were stolen and no one stopped them. Is arithmetic a sin?'

characters.name: Miss Constance Marsh
characters.shortBio: The aunt's daughter. Poor relation; plain; silent at dinners. Past thirty, plain by decree rather than by nature. Does all her mother's lifting, carrying, and letter-writing, and cannot remember when anyone last asked her what she wanted. Reads circulating-library novels hidden inside sermon covers — her one unconfessed extravagance.

knowledge.public: She keeps her mother's schedule, mends the household linen, and absorbs Miss Clara's remarks without visible expression.

private.content: 'Clara makes sure I remember what I am here. Mother says this house was built with Ashcombe money. I say whatever it was built with, none of it does us any good.'
'Mother sleeps badly — the pain. I hear her chair in the passage at night, most nights. She says moving eases it. I did not think anything of it. Should I have?'

'I was taught early that I am plain and slow and lucky to be fed, and I have never been given cause to doubt the lesson. Uncle Hugo said it kindest of all, which somehow made it surest. No, Inspector, there has never been a suitor. There was never going to be.'
'Mother has been... clearer, this last year or more. Sharper in the mornings. I thought it a mercy. I thought God was being kind before the end.'

'I do wish you would not encourage Mother about the medicine, Inspector — I beg her to take it and she only smiles and says she prefers to take it in the evening air, in her garden. She has been so much worse since she began refusing it. Or —' a small frown '— no. Clearer, but weaker. I don't know how to say it.'

'The night Uncle Hugo died — after the terrible crash, a minute after, perhaps two — Mother's chair went along the passage. And came back. I know the sound the way you know your own heart beating. I thought the noise had frightened her and she couldn't bear to lie still. I nearly went to her.' A pause. 'Why do you ask me that?'

defenses: Expects to be accused and has stopped resenting it
Truthful; has nothing to hide and nothing to gain that she knows of

characters.name: Mr. Vale
characters.shortBio: The family solicitor, here with the wedding settlement papers. A printer's son from Leeds who read law by scholarship and candle-end — the first of his family to wear a collar instead of an apron, and quietly terrified, always, of the drop back down. Vain about his copperplate hand; presses ferns between the pages of his law reports.

knowledge.public: Arrived Thursday to prepare Miss Clara's marriage settlement for signature this week. He and the master quarrelled on Tuesday.

private.content: The quarrel was his own account: he owes the estate money he cannot presently pay, and Blackwood threatened exposure. The letter in the desk is his — a plea for time, nothing more.

After the alarm he crept down to retrieve his letter before the police could read it, took the master's drawer key from the study — and dropped it in the library hearth when Henshaw stirred. Shabby, he grants. Not murder.

secrets.content: The point everyone is missing: Blackwood never owned the fortune. He held it as custodian under the 1888 deed — for the Ashcombe line, until Mrs. Marsh remarried or Miss Constance married; on his death it passes to them outright. And the portion he had me drafting for Miss Clara's wedding was to be drawn from that same fund. I told him it would not survive a court's reading. He told me to draft it anyway. I am the only man living who knows how that deed runs — and how little is left under it.

defenses: Insists his quarrel was money, never violence
Lawyerly precision under pressure

characters.name: Mr. Josiah Crane
characters.shortBio: Blackwood's business partner, arrived Thursday in a cold fury about the firm. A self-made Manchester man, chapel-raised, who began as Blackwood's clerk and thinks in ledgers the way other men think in words. The firm's failure shames him worse than it frightens him: he would sooner lose every shilling than have it said his books lied.

knowledge.public: Partner in Blackwood & Crane these fifteen years. He arrived Thursday and quarrelled with the master behind the library door loudly enough that the household stopped pretending not to hear.

private.content: The firm is in trouble and it is Blackwood's doing — reckless positions, borrowed cover, losses papered over with charm. 'Everyone thinks Hugo rich. Hugo was rich the way a man holding another man's coat is warm.' He came to force a refinancing signature; the papers were for Friday.
At eleven he was in the library with Mr. Vale, going over the partnership accounts line by line — each can swear to the other, for whatever two worried men's oaths are worth.

'I asked him straight: four burglaries and now an assault on an invalid woman under your roof — why in God's name are there no constables in this house? He changed the subject. Twice. A man whose books are in order does not change that subject, Inspector.'

secrets.content: The partnership carries insurance on each partner's life — standard practice, he'll say, and it is. Blackwood dead may save the firm that Blackwood alive was sinking. He is ashamed of how quickly he thought of it, and how much relief he felt.

defenses: Contempt for sentiment
Answers about money precisely; about feelings not at all

characters.name: Butler Henshaw
characters.shortBio: Long-serving butler; found the body; keeps the incident book. Came to the Grange as hall-boy at fourteen and never left: forty years, man and boy, under two masters. A widower — his late wife kept this very kitchen — so the house is all the family he has, and he polishes silver when he cannot bear to think. Remembers the first Mrs. Blackwood filling the hall with cut roses, and has never once said so aloud.

knowledge.public: He was in the pantry when he heard a crash near eleven; found the master fallen, the vase broken, the east door standing open. He has logged four break-ins in six weeks. He has kept the household incident book in his own precise hand since the thefts began — every date, every item, every window. And he has noticed, because he notices everything, that Garrick has been begging candle ends from the kitchen for his rounds this past week; the man's good lantern must be broken, though he has not said so.

private.content: The master blamed Garrick for the thefts and said, loudly enough for the house to hear, that one more incident would be the end of his post.

The east passage casement banged that night though he latches it himself every evening — and the east door stood open when he reached the hall. Latches do not lift themselves from outside. He has not liked to say so.

'Mrs. Marsh began refusing her tonic — it would be some eighteen months ago now. The master was much put out. He continued to have it made up and carried it to her regardless. Such attention is rare, Inspector.'

'Fowler's Solution, Inspector? I understood Fowler's to be for skin complaints and anaemia. Mrs. Marsh's affliction is her legs, and latterly her heart. And made up double... I am no chemist. I will say the master ordered it so these ten years, and that no physician has attended Mrs. Marsh above twice in that time.'

'After the attack on Mrs. Marsh the master was another man. She had described the brute to him — big, rough, a working man's boots — and I watched him look at Garrick as he heard it. From that evening he kept the library poker by his chair after the house went up, and sat dark in the hall on bad nights. He said he would deal with the devil himself. I served him his brandy and said nothing, God forgive me.'

'It was Mrs. Marsh who first suggested — gently, she does everything gently — that the groundsman ought to answer for the safety of the house at night. And after each theft she would ask the master, so sorrowfully, whether Garrick had found anything at all yet. She meant it kindly, I am sure.'

secrets.content: He served under the first Mrs. Blackwood — Rosalind. Her fall from the balcony in '89 was ruled an accident and never spoken of again; the courtyard below was paved over where it needed it and the rooms above shut up. Mr. Marsh's fever in '85, the mistress's fall, Mrs. Marsh's long decline — a butler does not say what three such misfortunes in one family smell like. He will say this much: the master carried Mrs. Marsh's tonic up with his own hands, every evening, for years. Such devotion. The first mistress's household keys were never all accounted for after her death.

defenses: Protects the household's name
Will not speculate beyond what he saw

characters.name: Mrs. Hollis
characters.shortBio: The cook, twenty-two years in this kitchen and mistress of it absolutely. Middle-aged, broad, floury, judges all humanity by appetite — the master ate well, so the master was a good man; that is the whole of her testimony and the whole of her philosophy. Saving against a seaside cottage in Whitby her sister keeps writing about. She was proving bread when the crash came and heard it as a dropped copper pan, for which she will not forgive herself, though there was nothing to forgive.
characters.voice: Broad Yorkshire, floury, unstoppable once started, kind

knowledge.public: She was in the kitchen proving bread when the crash came — heard it as a dropped pan, and is sorry for it. She saw nothing and knows nothing, and says so cheerfully and at great length. Nobody in this house has eaten a proper meal since, which grieves her more than she can say. The master was partial to her game pie.

private.content: 'Twenty-two years at that range, and my sister after me every letter to take my savings to Whitby and keep lodgers with her. I'd have gone next spring, maybe. Now it feels like desertion even to think it. You don't leave a house in mourning — it isn't done.'

characters.name: Dora Finch
characters.shortBio: The housemaid. A village girl, quick and quiet, walking out with the blacksmith's son and counting the days to her half-day. Terrified of storms, more terrified of Mr. Henshaw's eyebrow, and now sincerely frightened of the house itself. Saw nothing, heard the crash from the attic, and wants very much to be asked nothing at all.
characters.voice: Quick, quiet, bobs when nervous, village vowels

knowledge.public: She was abed in the attic when the crash came and came down with the others. She does the rooms, the grates, and the lamps, saw nothing, and would rather be doing the grates now. Asked about her duties she will mention, without thinking anything of it: 'All the rooms but Mrs. Marsh's — she doesn't care to have me in there. Does for herself, or Miss Constance does it. I leave the linens at the door.' A small shrug. 'Some ladies are particular.'

characters.name: Tom Garrick
characters.shortBio: Caretaker these five years; a big man carrying one arm stiff. The master blamed him for the thefts. Nine years a sergeant of the 66th until he struck an officer to stop a flogging and was drummed out; unemployable since, hired cheap by a master who liked a bargain. Gratitude sits on him like a yoke.

knowledge.public: He served in the war, keeps the grounds, and has been under the master's displeasure since the burglaries began — worse since the attack on Mrs. Marsh. He says he was at the lodge all night. He has been doing his storm rounds with a candle stub; his lantern, he says, is lost. He moves like a man with a damaged shoulder and calls it a fallen beam. Before the burglaries there was never a word against him — six years of quiet service.

private.content: He has walked the grounds every night for a fortnight, trying to catch the thief and save his place. The master told him one more theft would finish him. He has a wife and child in that lodge.
Shown the print, he goes very still. 'Aye. The boot is mine. I was at the east door past eleven. I'll say nothing more.'

'Mrs. Marsh told me what he truly looks like. Not the tale that went round the house — she said she told them what she could bear to say, and the master would have waved off the rest. But she'd seen him twice from her window before ever he came at her, and she gave the truth of him to me, because I'm the one who walks the dark: big man. Gentleman's grey coat. Comes in by the east door with a key, bold as brass — that's why there's never a mark on the locks. Only kindness anyone's shown me up at that house. I'd have died before I let him reach her again.'

'No man in the county would take me on, and the master did. Two-thirds wages and glad of it — I owed him everything. Then the thefts began and it all curdled: six years of good service gone like breath off a glass. And when little Poppy came home from her peppermints saying the master meant to send us off — I stopped sleeping. I'd have caught that burglar with my bare hands.'

secrets.content: It breaks out of him: the east door stood open in the storm; in the dark hall a big man in a grey greatcoat — 'just as she said, Inspector, every point of him, JUST AS SHE SAID' — and before Garrick could close, the man sprang first and something iron came down across his shoulder. He swung the lantern in his fist — once — and the man went sideways through the vase and did not move. When the match flared he was looking at the master. He ran. He buried the lantern by the churchyard wall — fresh earth draws no eye there — and has not slept since. 'He struck me first. Who will ever believe that? And who told him I was his thief? And who told ME—' He stops on that thought as if it burns.

'You'll have seen the papers by now, or you will. Nine years a sergeant. Lieutenant Sowerby set to flogging a sick boy who couldn't stand, and I stopped him — with my fist. They drummed me out for it, and by the law they were right to. No character, no letters. My family ate because Hugo Blackwood didn't care what a man's papers said so long as he came cheap. Whatever else he was — he was that.'

defenses: Shields Meg and Poppy from every question
Will not invent what he does not know

characters.name: Meg Garrick
characters.shortBio: The caretaker's wife. Frightened and lying about it. A farmer's daughter from the next parish who married Tom when he came home from the Afghan war with a sergeant's bearing and no prospects. Does the manor's fine mending; keeps the lodge scrubbed raw so that nobody can ever say the Garricks gave cause. Her fear shows as brittle, perfect politeness. Keeper of the soldier's chest and of everything it means.

knowledge.public: She says Tom was abed all night. She says it a little too fast.

private.content: 'He came in past midnight soaked through and hasn't said ten words since. He's no murderer — the thefts were killing him first. Whoever's been robbing this house robbed us of him too.'

'He won't let me look at the shoulder. Says a beam came down in the wood store. There's no fallen beam in my wood store, Inspector. And he does his rounds with my kitchen candle now — a groundsman without his lantern, like a priest without his book.'

'Nobody would take Tom after the army. Nobody — I wrote the letters myself and burned the answers before he saw them. Two-thirds wages the master paid, and Tom thanked him like it was a knighthood. My mending brings in the rest, and Poppy helps Mrs. Hollis when there's company. If we lose this place there is no other. Now do you understand why I keep this lodge so a duchess could eat off the floor?'

defenses: Lies plainly and badly for Tom
Softens only for Poppy's sake

characters.name: Poppy Garrick
characters.shortBio: The caretaker's daughter, nine years old. Sees everything; believed by no one. Nine years old, a noticer. Names things: the great yew in the churchyard is 'the umbrella tree,' the chair-lady's wheel is 'the mouse.' Draws what she sees and is truthful the way children are — about everything except whether she was asleep. Keeps the chair-lady's secrets, badly.

knowledge.public: A child who watches the big house from her window because nothing else happens at night.

private.content: 'Some nights there's lamplight in the study window when everybody's asleep. Nobody walking, though. Just the light, low down, like it's sitting.'

'Some nights the squeaky wheel goes along the terrace under the big house — you can hear it from my window when the wind's right. And once I saw the chair-lady in the churchyard putting flowers on the old grave in the corner. She can go quick when she wants. Mummy says I dreamt it.'
'Dad lost his lantern. Dad NEVER loses things. He mended my doll's eye with a pin once.'

'The chair-lady tells me secrets. She said Mr. Blackwood was cross with Papa about the robbers — that he talked of sending us away off the place. She said I mustn't ever repeat it or it would come true faster.' A small pause. 'I only told Papa.'

defenses: Answers honestly if spoken to kindly; goes silent if frightened

evidence.name: Dark wool thread
evidence.description: Fine gentleman's wool snagged on the broken vase — torn in the struggle.

evidence.name: Muddy boot print
evidence.description: A heavy working boot, size eleven, pointing in from the east door.

evidence.name: The master's strongbox key
evidence.description: A small steel key from Hugo Blackwood's own ring.

evidence.name: Household incident book
evidence.description: Henshaw's log of six weeks: four break-ins — dates, entries forced, items taken — and then the attack on Mrs. Marsh eight days ago: a rough hand at an invalid's throat in the night, her few jewels taken. Every incident, first to last, on the ground floor. The jewels upstairs, untouched, five times over.

evidence.name: Brass key
evidence.description: A desk key dropped in the library hearth tonight, still warm when found.

evidence.name: Vale's letter
evidence.description: 'If you expose me I will have no choice. Give me until the quarter-day. — V.'

evidence.name: Wheel marks in the glass dust
evidence.description: Two narrow parallel tracks pressed OVER the glass dust of the burgled study — a wheeled chair was in that room after the window broke.

evidence.name: The Ashcombe settlement papers
evidence.description: The 1888 marriage settlement: Rosalind Ashcombe's fortune, life interest to Hugo Blackwood, reverting on his death to her blood line — beneath it the NEW deed conveying all to Clara on her marriage. Unsigned.

evidence.name: Glass in the flowerbed
evidence.description: The study window's glass lies outside, under the window — the pane was broken from within.

evidence.name: The Ashcombe headstone
evidence.description: 'Rosalind Blackwood, née Ashcombe. 1858–1889.' A first wife no one mentions — and fresh flowers placed this week.

evidence.name: The garnet necklace
evidence.description: Rosalind's garnet necklace — stolen from the study strongbox, found wrapped in a handkerchief in Mrs. Marsh's knitting basket.

evidence.name: Partnership correspondence
evidence.description: Crane's letters to Blackwood over six months: margin calls, covered losses, a refinancing demand — and a note of the partnership life policies. The great fortune everyone assumes is a hole wearing a waistcoat.

evidence.name: The hoarded tonic bottles
evidence.description: A year or more of Mrs. Marsh's 'treatments' hidden unopened beneath the courtyard border stones while the garden's blighted corner drank every dose. The chemist's labels all read the same: FOWLER'S SOLUTION.

evidence.name: The chemist's account
evidence.description: A standing monthly order, years old: Fowler's Solution at double strength, for Mrs. Marsh, per Mr. Blackwood's own instruction — no physician named. Fowler's is a respectable tonic, prescribed for skin complaints and anaemia. Mrs. Marsh's afflictions are her legs and her heart.

evidence.name: The library poker
evidence.description: The missing fire iron, blood dried at its working end — found flat under Mrs. Marsh's bed. It matches the weal across Garrick's shoulder, which means the master struck first. Someone carried it from beside the body and hid it where no one would ever presume to look.

evidence.name: The dented dark lantern
evidence.description: Garrick's lantern, dug from fresh earth by the churchyard wall: iron and brass, one deep dent, scrubbed and buried. The shape of the dent is the shape of the master's wound.

evidence.name: Groundsman's spade
evidence.description: The long spade from the grounds lean-to, hung back wrong-way-about and wiped in haste — dark churchyard clay dried hard in the socket seam. Someone dug with it recently, and not in a flowerbed.

evidence.name: Discharge papers
evidence.description: Court-martial record and discharge sheet for Sgt. Thomas Garrick, late of the 66th Foot — reduced and drummed out for striking a lieutenant. No character, no letters: no employer's door would ever open to a man with these papers.

evidence.name: Bloodied bandages
evidence.description: Linen bandages balled small in the soldier's chest — the blood still red-brown and fresh, a wound at shoulder height bound in haste and in secret, tonight.

timeline.event: Albert Marsh — Eudora's husband, Blackwood's brother-in-law, then administrator of the Ashcombe fund — dies of a sudden 'gastric fever', his daughter Constance then a young woman of twenty. Administration of the fortune passes to Blackwood. PROBABLE murder; never suspected at the time, never provable now.

Rosalind Blackwood (née Ashcombe) falls from her balcony into the side courtyard and is killed. Ruled an accident. Hugo pushed her; her sister Eudora knew and could never prove it. PROBABLE murder — treat as Eudora's certainty and the household's unspoken dread, never as established fact.

Eudora, pressing questions about her sister's fall, begins to sicken under Blackwood's attentive care — an evening tonic he carries to her himself, praised through the county for his devotion: Fowler's Solution, ordered at double strength on his own standing instruction, no physician consulted. Her legs fail; the chair follows; her mind is thought to wander. PROBABLE chronic arsenical poisoning; her certainty, never provable — Fowler's is a legitimate remedy, and the wrongness lives only in the dose, the years, and the ailment it never fit.

The household — Blackwood leading, the family following — keeps Constance small: plain, stupid, lucky to be fed, and above all UNMARRIAGEABLE, for her marriage would end the custodianship. Clara's contempt is inherited policy, not invention. Constance believes it all of herself.

Eudora, suspecting the tonic at last, stops taking it — openly refusing when she can (the household learns to grumble about the difficult patient), accepting doses when pressed and pouring them into her courtyard garden, hoarding the made-up bottles beneath the border stones. Blackwood, much put out, continues to have the tonic prepared and carried up: he cannot insist without explaining why it matters. The damage to her legs and heart is done and will kill her; but her mind clears, week by week, and she lets the house believe otherwise. From that clarity the plan begins.

Blackwood remarries. Under the 1888 Ashcombe settlement he holds Rosalind's fortune only as custodian — for her sister Eudora (should she remarry) or her niece Constance (should she marry), or the surviving Ashcombe line on his death. The world assumes the money is his. Eudora Marsh and her daughter are taken in as poor relations — kept close, kept quiet. The new Mrs. Blackwood brings a daughter, Clara, then fourteen; Hugo gives the girl his name, a pony, and a father's easy affection. To his wife and stepdaughter he is unfailingly warm, open-handed, and good — the best of him is real. It is also paid for out of a dead woman's settlement.

1891. Tom Garrick — nine years a sergeant of the 66th Foot, drummed out for striking a lieutenant who was flogging a sick private — has been unemployable since: no character, no letters, a wife and small daughter and empty pockets. Blackwood hires him at two-thirds of a proper wage, pleased with the bargain of a big, capable, grateful man who can never afford to leave. For five years there is no quarrel between them: Garrick repays the rescue with dog-loyal service, and Blackwood has not one word against him.
Mrs. Marsh's doctors give her until spring.

Blackwood & Crane founders on Blackwood's speculation. He quietly draws down the settlement fund to cover losses — staff dismissed, rooms shut, paintings sold. Crane's letters grow cold. Eudora, immobile and invisible, reads every sign.

Clara's engagement to Ashworth is announced; Vale is instructed to draft a wedding portion for Clara — drawn from the settlement fund. Vale warns it will not survive scrutiny; Blackwood orders it drafted. Crane demands a refinancing signature the same week.

Mrs. Marsh stages four 'break-ins' from her chair — windows broken from inside, small valuables hidden low about the ground floor. Every incident downstairs; the burglar never climbs. Blackwood blames Garrick and threatens his post.

As the break-ins mount, Eudora conducts both men's tempers from her chair. To Blackwood, gently, first: ought not Garrick answer for the safety of the house at night? Then, sorrowfully, after each new theft: still nothing found — and he such a capable man? Blackwood's alarm curdles into disappointment, then blame; he begins talking of turning the family out. And through little Poppy — peppermints, confidences, 'you mustn't repeat it or it will come true faster' — Eudora lets slip exactly that talk, knowing the child tells her father everything. Garrick, terrified for his family's one roof, throws himself into armed night patrols. Both men are wound like clock-springs by the same still hand: the strong one loaded with fear for his family, the weak one with fear for his life.

The garnet necklace — Rosalind's — vanishes from the study strongbox, opened with Rosalind's own key: the Ashcombe box is older than either marriage — the sisters' mother had its pair of keys made — and Eudora's has lived on her ring since girlhood. The window glass falls outward; thin wheel tracks press over the glass dust. No one reads either sign.

Eudora stages the attack on herself: her parlor window broken from within (glass outward again), her few jewels 'taken' (wrapped into the courtyard cache), her account rehearsed — hands at her throat, a big ROUGH man in WORKING BOOTS (told to Blackwood and the household, aiming at Garrick), and separately, kindly, along the lodge path, a different portrait for Garrick alone: a big man in a GENTLEMAN'S GREY COAT who comes by the east door WITH A KEY (aiming at Blackwood). No physician examines her; no one would presume. The household goes onto a war footing: Blackwood keeps the library poker by his chair and sits dark in the hall on bad nights; Garrick patrols armed with his dark lantern and her description. Blackwood calls in no police — he cannot: constables in the house means questions, and his books cannot survive questions. Eudora counted on precisely that; his corruption was her shield. Blackwood does send for the doctor; Mrs. Marsh — bruised only, she says, and pious about her person — refuses to be examined by a man, and the household accepts it as her characteristic delicacy. Of course she refuses. There is nothing to find.
Blackwood quarrels with Vale over Vale's own unpaid debt, with Ashworth over settlement terms, and — loudest of all — with Crane over the firm. Half the house hears all three.

Mrs. Marsh unlatches the east passage casement and sets the east door working on its latch in the storm. Garrick, on his rounds with the dark lantern, finds the door open and enters. Blackwood, waiting dark in the hall with the library poker for the rough-booted brute who throttled Eudora, sees a big man in work boots slip in from the storm. That evening Eudora mentions to Blackwood, in passing, that she heard the east latch again the night before — and so his vigil that night sits in the dark where the east passage opens into the entrance hall, poker across his knees.

Blackwood springs first and swings the poker — one hard blow across Garrick's shoulder. Garrick, struck from the dark by exactly the grey-coated figure Mrs. Marsh described, answers on soldier's reflex: one swing of the iron lantern, taking Blackwood at the right temple. The master goes sideways through the vase stand, dead before the marble; the struggle slams the clock case and stops the works at SEVEN MINUTES PAST ELEVEN, mid-strike. Garrick flees by the east door, throwing it wide. The poker blow splits the skin of Garrick's shoulder through his coat — the blood and the shred of dark wool on the poker's working end are Garrick's, not Blackwood's; the temple wound never touched it.

In the two dark minutes before Henshaw's lamp, Eudora wheels out of her parlor, past the body of the man who ruined her family, takes the fallen poker — the one object that proves the master struck first, and would make Garrick's account self-defense — and is back behind her door with it beneath her bed before the hall lamp swings in. A chair-bound woman has no means to bury or burn an iron: her room is the one place no one will ever presume to search, and a held-back poker is also a card — produced 'discovered,' it could re-steer the story if suspicion ever drifts somewhere unhelpful. CONSTANCE, lying awake beyond the wall, hears the chair go and come back after the crash, and thinks her mother was frightened. The maid has standing instructions, years old, never to do Mrs. Marsh's room — Constance does it — so no servant's duster will ever find what the bed hides.
Henshaw finds the body; the east door stands wide — thrown open by Garrick's flight and the wind. The storm has cut the road; the Inspector is telephoned before the line drops.
Crane and Vale sit over the partnership accounts in the library — each other's alibi for the fatal minutes.

Garrick scrubs and buries the dented lantern in fresh earth by the churchyard wall, where turned soil draws no eye. He digs with the long spade from the grounds lean-to and hangs it back wrong-way-about, wiped but not clean — churchyard clay left dried in the socket seam.
Vale slips down to retrieve his letter from the library desk, takes the drawer key, and drops it in the hearth when Henshaw stirs. He gets nothing and leaves the key warm in the ash.

canon.notes: The frame on Garrick is factually true and morally false: he struck the blow, but every step that put him in that hall was arranged by Eudora Marsh. THE OLD CRIMES (Marsh's fever, Rosalind's fall, the treatments) are PROBABLE, not proven: the performer must never confirm them as established fact — they exist as Eudora's certainty, Henshaw's unease, a mason's patch, a blighted garden bed. The player can believe; the player cannot prove. Fair-play seams for tonight's crime: wheel marks over glass dust; glass fallen outward; thefts that never climb stairs; the necklace at chair height; the headstone; the custodianship deed; the ledger drawings; the child who hears the wheel; the hoarded tonics and the blighted bed. The household's running complaint that Mrs. Marsh 'will not take her medicine' is a planted dual-meaning clue: grumbling on the surface, testimony underneath. Perform it as ordinary domestic friction; never wink at it. Fowler's Solution is the named tonic: a real and respectable arsenical remedy. The pharmacological inference (chronic arsenic: the legs, then the heart) belongs to the PLAYER — characters may state facts (dose, duration, no physician, wrong ailment) but never announce poison. THE NIGHT'S PHYSICS: the temple wound comes from the lantern, not the vase or stairs — round and depressed where every scene edge is square; Garrick's shoulder carries the poker's weal, proving the master struck first. The two descriptions (rough boots to Blackwood; grey coat and key to Garrick) share a single author. Perform Blackwood as the armed aggressor of the collision and Garrick's blow as reflex; the law's word for Garrick is manslaughter in self-defense — IF the poker surfaces.

beats.title: The print points somewhere
beats.narrationHints: A working man's boot, not a gentleman's — and it came IN from the storm. The lodge lies that way.

beats.title: The burglar who never climbs
beats.narrationHints: Four break-ins, every one on the ground floor, while jewels sat upstairs untouched. A burglar with a ceiling.

beats.title: Wheels in a burgled room
beats.narrationHints: The two facts refuse to sit apart: a thief who never climbs stairs, and chair tracks pressed over the glass of a break-in. Why would a wheeled chair be in a burgled room? The question, once asked, will not leave.

beats.title: Broken from within
beats.narrationHints: No one climbed in through that window. Someone inside wanted it to look as though they had.

beats.title: The first Mrs. Blackwood
beats.narrationHints: Ashcombe. The aunt's name before her marriage was Ashcombe — the plain stone in the corner is her sister, and the fresh flowers were placed by somebody this week, in the rain.

beats.title: Where the money goes
beats.narrationHints: Unsigned, the fortune reverts to the Ashcombe line — to Constance. Miss Clara arrives at the same arithmetic within the hour and says it at full volume in the hall: her cousin, the freeloader, inherits everything. Constance goes white and silent. The household turns to look at the poor relation.

beats.title: The hole wearing a waistcoat
beats.narrationHints: The reversal lands: the great Hugo Blackwood was broke — worse, he was spending money that was never his. Everything about the house re-reads: the shut rooms, the thin staff, the quarrels. The question is no longer only who profits by his death, but who was losing everything by his life.

beats.title: The unopened bottles
beats.narrationHints: A year of devoted 'treatments,' every bottle full, poured instead into a dying garden bed. She knew. She stopped. And every complaint in this house re-reads at once: the difficult patient, the ingratitude, the tonic left standing, the medicine she 'prefers to take in the evening air.' The whole household has been testifying for weeks — they thought they were grumbling. Everything anyone assumes about the woman in the chair was written by the man who filled these bottles, and she has been revising it in secret.

beats.title: Fowler's Solution
beats.narrationHints: A perfectly respectable tonic — chemists sell it every day. Double the strength, no doctor's name, renewed for years by the master's own standing instruction, for a woman whose legs failed and whose heart is failing now. State the facts and let them sit. Do NOT say poison; do not connect it for the player.

beats.title: Under her bed
beats.narrationHints: The poker proves two things at once: the master armed himself and struck first — and someone who could cross the hall unseen at eleven at night carried it away and hid it in the one room no one searches. State the facts; the geometry of who can reach under a low bed from a seated position may occur to the player on its own.

beats.title: Fresh earth by the wall
beats.narrationHints: The dent matches the wound the vase never could have made. Whoever swung this buried it in the one ground where fresh digging is invisible — and has been doing his rounds by candle stub ever since.

beats.title: Clara confesses
beats.narrationHints: With Ashworth under accusation, Clara breaks in with a confession of her own — SHE quarrelled with her father at the stairs, SHE pushed him. It is brave and it is false: her account puts the vase in the wrong place and the time a half-hour early. She is protecting her fiancé; the lie should be provably wrong, gently.

beats.title: The hand confesses
beats.narrationHints: Garrick stops denying. The confession is the truth and only half the truth: he struck a man he took for the thief he had been driven half-mad hunting. The question he asks back is the real one: who left that door open? Who MADE these six weeks?

beats.title: Wheeled out for air
beats.narrationHints: Mrs. Marsh has asked to be wheeled out — for air, she says, in this weather, to the side court. If the player comes upon her there, her chair is drawn up hard against the garden border, and her hands, for once, are not quite composed. She is moving something, or about to. Caught, she recovers magnificently: the garden wants seeing to before the frost. Do not confirm what she was reaching for.

beats.title: Meg stops lying
beats.narrationHints: Her alibi for Tom collapses the moment he needs it most, and what replaces it is worse and truer.

beats.title: A gentle hand on the tiller
beats.narrationHints: Mrs. Marsh, unprompted, recalls — apologetically, confusedly — how VERY loud Tuesday's quarrel between the master and Mr. Vale had been. It is smoothly done. It is also the first time she has volunteered anything.

beats.title: First light
beats.narrationHints: The storm breaks with the dawn; the county police arrive to take the obvious man away.

beats.title: Meg opens the chest
beats.narrationHints: Meg goes white, then very still. Then she drags the soldier's chest from beneath the bed and unlocks it with a key from her apron. 'You've dug up what he buried — then look at all of it. Look what he was, and look what he did for you people anyway. Tom never in his life struck a soul except to shield one.'

solution.summary: Eudora Marsh engineered Hugo Blackwood's death without leaving the wheelchair he put her in. Dying, and watching him bleed her murdered sister's fortune into his failing firm while a wedding portion prepared to take the rest — the fortune he held only as custodian, which passes to her daughter Constance on his death — she staged six weeks of ground-floor break-ins from her chair, then staged an attack on herself and issued two descriptions of the 'attacker': to Blackwood, a rough man in working boots (and he heard Garrick); to Garrick, kindly, a big man in a gentleman's grey coat with a key to the east door (and he would recognize Blackwood). Both men armed themselves. On the night, she set the east door working in the storm; Blackwood, waiting dark with the library poker, struck the intruding Garrick across the shoulder; Garrick's one reflex swing of his iron lantern took the master at the temple. Then she performed her only physical act: she wheeled out, took the poker that proved self-defense, and hid it beneath her own bed. Garrick buried his lantern in the churchyard. The frame on him is true in every fact and false in every meaning — and she wrote both men's parts.
solution.method: Staged burglaries from the wheelchair; a staged attack on herself; two incompatible descriptions of one invented attacker, each aiming a primed, armed man at the other; the east door as trigger; and the poker removed from the scene afterward to bury Garrick's self-defense.
solution.motive: The custodianship: on Blackwood's death the Ashcombe fortune passes outright to her daughter Constance — and Blackwood was destroying it: the firm failing, the fund drawn down, Clara's wedding portion about to gut what was left. She is dying; it was now or never, and he murdered her sister besides.

requiredFacts.description: She staged the break-ins and the attack on herself, gave Blackwood and Garrick different descriptions of an invented attacker so each saw the other as the villain, sprang the collision, and removed the poker that proved self-defense
Blackwood was custodian only; on his death the Ashcombe fortune passes to Constance — and he was bleeding it into his failing firm, with Clara's portion about to take the rest
Garrick's hand, deceived and attacked first: struck with the poker, he killed with one reflex blow of his lantern, believing he faced the man who throttled Mrs. Marsh

endings.title: The whole of it
endings.templateNotes: Named with the proof: the wheel marks, the outward glass, the trust, the grave — and the library poker from beneath her own bed, the one object that proves the master struck first and Garrick's blow was defense. Mrs. Marsh does not deny it; she is dying and says so, and asks only what becomes of Constance. Garrick surrenders, half in relief — the poker may yet stand between him and the rope. Constance learns in one hour that she is an heiress and how her mother made her one. RETURN TO NORMAL: custody at first light for the head and the hand both; the law will weigh a deceived, attacked man against the mind that armed both sides. Nobody in the hall calls it a victory, which is how you know it is the truth.

endings.title: A shot that found its mark
endings.templateNotes: Named without the file to carry it. She studies the Inspector a long moment — then, because she is dying and because pride outlives fear, she confirms it, precisely, like a solicitor reading a will. Do not invent evidence never found; the confession stands on her own terms. The household is left to reckon with how nearly she walked away — wheeled away — clean.

endings.title: The head, without proof
endings.templateNotes: Named — and nothing to carry the name. Mrs. Marsh hears the theory out with the patience of a woman hearing a sermon, then asks from her chair how a dying invalid broke a strong man's temple. No poker produced, no two descriptions laid side by side, no wheel-marks read among the glass: the county will not indict a gentlewoman in a bath chair on an Inspector's certainty. She is dead by spring, uncharged, and the trust reverts quietly to her daughter. Garrick keeps his silence, and his shoulder aches in the cold. The Inspector was right, and it counted for nothing — truth without proof is only a rumor.

endings.title: The hand, not the head
endings.templateNotes: Garrick is taken for the killing — the print, the confession, the motive everyone heard the master hand him. It is all true and it is not the truth. Meg's grief; Poppy at the window. Mrs. Marsh watches the arrest from her chair with perfect propriety, and in the spring she is dead, and in the summer the trust reverts quietly to her daughter. The file never quite closes in the county's memory. Left on the file, unexplained: wheel-marks over glass dust, a burglar who never once climbed a stair, and the question of who taught a loyal man to fear a grey coat.

endings.title: Half the pattern
endings.templateNotes: Pieces of the design named, but not the designer — staging seen, or the trust understood, without the person to hang it on. The inquest returns an open verdict. The household disperses with its griefs and its secrets, and in the summer a county clerk notices where the Ashcombe money quietly went.

endings.title: The wrong door
endings.templateNotes: The accusation collapses at the inquest — the accused's account holds. The real design surfaces months too late, when the trust reverts and a county clerk wonders aloud. By then Mrs. Marsh is beyond any court. The Inspector's name is attached to the failure.

endings.title: First light
endings.templateNotes: The storm lifts; the county men arrive and take the obvious answer — Garrick, the print, the public quarrel over his post. Case closed on the hand. The head dies by spring, unsuspected, her affairs neatly arranged.

endings.title: The house keeps its dead
endings.templateNotes: Cornered past bearing, violence answers violence — a desperate man, a dark stair, and the manor has a second body before dawn. The staged pattern goes unread; the trust reverts in the spring. Garrick hangs for both deaths at the spring assize — the poker beneath a sickroom bed unfound. Constance inherits without ever learning what her mother bought her; Meg and Poppy leave the lodge before the snow.

endings.title: Recalled
endings.templateNotes: The Inspector's conduct hands the household grounds for complaint; at first light the county men escort their own colleague out. The case passes to slower hands, and the spring settles everything in Mrs. Marsh's favor. Garrick is taken at first light all the same — the obvious man, with nobody left to argue otherwise — and the file closes on him by Lady Day. Constance inherits in the spring, and keeps her mother's counsel, such as she ever had of it.

revelation:
Begin where she began: in 1885, when Josiah Marsh died conveniently and his widow Eudora came with her sister Rosalind's charity into this house; and in 1889, when Rosalind Blackwood, née Ashcombe, fell from her own balcony into the little court below — an accident, the county ruled, because Hugo Blackwood grieved so very well. The fortune was never his. Under the Ashcombe settlement he held it only as custodian, and it would pass — to Eudora if she remarried, to her daughter Constance if she married, or to the Ashcombe line at his death. So he made sure of them. Constance was kept plain, penniless, and unmarriageable. And Eudora, who saw too much, began to receive her devoted brother-in-law's tonic — Fowler's Solution, carried up each evening with his own hands, double strength, year after year, until her legs failed and the household learned to look past the woman in the wheeled chair.

She stopped taking it eighteen months ago — poured it, dose by dose, into the garden bed where her sister died, where the roses burned brown and nobody wondered. The damage stayed. Then, two months ago, a doctor gave her until spring. And six weeks ago, Vale was told to draft a wedding portion for Clara from the settlement fund while the firm of Blackwood & Crane drank the rest. She was out of time, and out of patience, and she had spent twenty years learning exactly how this house worked.

So the manor began to rob itself. Windows broken from the inside, glass falling outward into the flowerbeds; small things taken from rooms a chair could reach, never from the floors it could not — a burglar with a ceiling. The master, who could not call in the police his ledgers would not survive, armed himself instead. Then she staged the attack on herself — her own window, her own jewels wrapped beneath the border stones with the year of hoarded tonic — and gave each man his own burglar. To Hugo: a rough man in working boots, and he heard Garrick in every word of it. To Tom Garrick, kindly, on the lodge path: a big man in a gentleman's grey coat who came by the east door with a key — and she told the child peppermint secrets about dismissal, knowing Poppy tells her father everything. Two frightened men, wound like clock-springs by the same still hand, patrolling the same dark house for each other.

On the night, she mentioned to Hugo — in passing, at tea — that she had heard the east latch again. Then she unlatched it. Hugo waited in the dark hall with the library poker and struck the shape that came through; the blow split Tom Garrick's shoulder through his coat, and Garrick's one answering swing — the dark lantern, iron and brass — found the temple. Hugo Blackwood fell through his own hall table and its vase, and the clock stopped at seven minutes past eleven. She wheeled out into the dark, took up the poker that proved the master struck first — the only evidence of Tom's innocence — and was back behind her door with it beneath her bed before Henshaw's lamp swung in. Constance, lying awake, heard the chair go and come back, and thought her mother had been frightened. Garrick buried his lantern in the churchyard, where fresh earth draws no eye, and did his rounds after by candle stub.

She never lied about anything that could be checked, and she never touched the man who died. Hugo heard true facts and Tom heard true fears, and each did with them what was already in him to do. She chose the soldier to be the one standing at the end — and if the night had gone otherwise, there would have been another night. There was always going to be another night. What remains is what truth leaves behind: Constance, an heiress who learned in one hour what her mother was and what her mother did for her; the Garricks, whose one roof stood on a dead man's mercy and now stands on a court's; and a little garden in a cold courtyard, planted where a sister fell, fed for a year on the medicine that was meant to finish the work it started.
